text:
Admission Date:  1995-01-27       Discharge Date:  1993-09-12

Date of Birth:   1995-08-04       Sex:  M

Service:

HISTORY OF THE PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 55-year-old
male with a history of a traumatic brain injury and seizure
disorder, obstructive sleep apnea, and chronic obstructive
pulmonary disease, as well as reported coronary artery
disease, who was admitted to the hospital yesterday from the
nursing home, where he was noted to be hypoxic to 76% on room
air with decreased responsiveness.  In the emergency
department, he was noted to have diffuse rhonchi on
examination and chest x-ray revealing right and left lobar
pneumonia.  The patient was transferred to the floor and
started on antibiotics for treatment of a possible aspiration
pneumonia.

Initial ABG was 7.30, 90, and 67.  The patient was alert and
awake.  Later that evening, the patient was noted to be less
responsive.  He was lethargic and essentially unresponsive to
even a deep sternal rub, although did show some mild
withdrawal this painful stimuli.  According to the nursing
staff, the mental status change had occurred over
approximately a-one hour period.  Fingerstick blood sugars
were within normal limits and the vital signs were not
significantly changed from admission.  Of note:  The patient
was still on a Prednisone taper from a recent admission at
the time of arrival.  He had been discharged earlier this
month for pneumonia and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
exacerbation on Prednisone, Levaquin, and Flagyl.  Also of
note, the patient had not received his phenobarbital, which
he takes chronically for history of seizures for the past two
days.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Traumatic brain injury in 2010 complicated by a seizure
disorder status post right temporal lobe lobectomy.
2.  Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, not on home
oxygen.  Baseline pCO2 have been 65 to 75 in the past.
3.  The patient does have a history of tobacco, quiting in
2010.
4.  History of congestive heart failure with ejection
fraction of approximately 45% by recent echocardiogram, mild
left ventricular hypertrophy and global RV free-wall
hypokinesis.
5.  History of obstructive sleep apnea.  The patient has not
tolerated CPAP.
6.  Chronic aspiration pneumonia.  The patient has previously
had a trach and PEG.  Last summer, the patient had a repeat
swallowing evaluation during which he was able to tolerate
soft solids and subsequently underwent removal of the trach
and PEG an allowed to take POs primarily for quality of life
issues.
7.  Coronary artery disease status post myocardial
infarction.  There was no evidence of prior catheterization
or stress test results on the computer.
8.  History or Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus
and vancomycin-resistant enterococcus by report.
9.  Chronic urinary tract infections.
10. Cataracts and glaucoma.
11. Chronic hypothermia with baseline temperature of 93 to
94.
12. Chronic bradycardia and hypotension, baseline heart rate
in the 50s and 60s, both of which have been attributed to a
central process, status post brain injury.
13. History of mild dysplastic syndrome.
14. History of chronic thrombocytopenia attributed to
myelodysplastic syndrome.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:
1.  Aspirin 81 mg p.o.q.d.
2.  Lasix 20 mg p.o.q.d.
3.  Levaquin 500 mg p.o.q.d.
4.  Flagyl 500 mg p.o. t.i.d.
5.  Singular 10 mg p.o.q.d.
6.  Colace 100 mg p.o.b.i.d.
7.  Flovent two puffs b.i.d.
8.  Haldol 2.5 mg p.o.b.i.d. currently being held.
9.  Alphagan ophthalmic drops to the left eye t.i.d.
10. Neurontin 300 mg t.i.d.
11. Lamictal 150 mg p.o.q.d.
12. Phenobarbital 60 mg p.o.q.d.; 90 mg p.o.q.h.s.
13. Prevacid 15 mg p.o.q.d.
14. Prednisone taper being weaned from the prior admission,
currently at 20 mg p.o.q.d.
15. Combivent two puffs q.4h.
16. Xalatan ophthalmic solution, 0.005% drops, one drop to
the left eye q.h.s.

ALLERGIES:  Allergies include the following:  TAPE, ATIVAN,
DILANTIN, OXACILLIN, TEGRETOL, SEAFOOD, CODEINE, AND
MEROPENEM.  The patient most recently developed a rash to
MEROPENEM on a prior hospitalization and it was promptly
discontinued.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  Admission physical examination to the
Medical Intensive Care Unit revealed the following:  Vital
signs:  Temperature 92.3 axillary, blood pressure 126/70,
pulse 51, respiratory rate 24, oxygen saturation 91% on two
liters.  GENERAL:  The patient is nontoxic, but chronically
ill-appearing in no acute distress.  HEENT:  Pupils are noted
to be deviated to the left.  This later resolved and they
were noted to be deviated to the right and then ultimately
midline.  Oropharynx was clear without lesions.  Tongue
midline.  NECK:  No JVD, supple.  No appreciable
lymphadenopathy.  CARDIOVASCULAR:  Regular rate and rhythm,
distant heart sounds, normal S1 and S2.  LUNGS:  Lungs
revealed diffuse rhonchi with decreased breath sounds at the
bases bilaterally.  ABDOMEN:  Soft and distended.
EXTREMITIES:  1+ edema in the lower extremities at the ankle
bilaterally.  NEUROLOGICAL:  Eyes initially noted to be
deviated to the left.  The patient was nonresponsive to
verbal stimuli with mild response to sternal rub.  The
patient does make some spontaneous movements of his
extremities with no obvious focal neurological deficit.

LABORATORY DATA:  Labs on admission to the Medical Intensive
Care Unit revealed the following:  White blood count 4.8,
hematocrit 30.9, baseline of 29 to 30.  Platelet count 46,
with baseline of 60 at 110, sodium of 150, potassium 4.6,
chloride 107, bicarbonate 32, BUN 23, creatinine 0.8, glucose
95.  ABG on admission was noted was 7.30, 90, and 67.
Subsequent ABGs on the floor, most recently, revealed 7.26,
83 and 65.  Urinalysis was negative.  Blood cultures and
urine cultures were pending.  Chest x-ray showed a right
middle lobe and left lower lobe pneumonia, no evidence of
congestive heart failure.  EKG showed normal sinus rhythm
with a slightly prolonged Q-T interval and T-wave inversions,
V1 through V6, 1 and L, no Qs.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  (by system)

#1.  NEUROLOGICAL:  The patient presented to the Medical
Intensive Care Unit with acute mental status change.  The
patient was found be essentially unresponsive with no
obvious focal neurologic deficit on the floor.  We considered
a broad differential in evaluating his mental status change.
The patient was urgently evaluated in the Medical Intensive
Care Unit on the date of admission by the Neurology Service
who felt that the most likely etiology was a postictal
period.  They felt that the patient may have seized secondary
to having had his phenobarbital held for the past two days.
However, EEG done on the day of admission showed no acute
process and actually was somewhat improved from his baseline
generalized slowing.  The patient had no suggestion of active
seizure activity during this hospitalization.  We felt that
CO2 narcosis could be contributing, given that the patient
has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with overlying
pneumonia.  The PCO2 were running higher than his baseline,
although the patient had had an equally high pCO2 on
admission without associated mental status depression.  The
patient was continued on steroids initially, IV
hydrocortisone and ultimately PO prednisone, as well as
bronchodilator therapy.

On 1989-10-02, the patient was intubated for airway
protection.  We felt that he was unable to protect his airway
with his heavy secretions given his depressed mental status.
There had been no acute decompensation from a respiratory
standpoint.  The patient was noted to be more arousable over
the next day to day and one half and was actually fighting
the vent, although he was never awake and alert.  He was
noted to be moving all extremities and he tried to self
extubate.  He was ultimately extubated on 1979-03-18.  ABGs
pre-extubation and postextubation were not significantly
changed in terms of oxygenation and ventilation.  The patient
was also evaluated by the Endocrine Service for his
persistent bradycardia, relative hypotension, and
hypothermia.  From previous admissions, the patient was noted
to have a baseline core-body temperature of approximately 93
to 94, which had been attributed to a central process, status
post a traumatic brain injury.  TSH on this admission was
within normal limits and T4 was low.  We asked the Endocrine
Service to evaluate him for possible panhypopituitarism
versus secondary hypothyroidism in the setting of his
traumatic brain injury.  On further evaluation, the Endocrine
Service felt that the most likely diagnosis was sick
euthyroid syndrome.  However, several followup endocrine
studies were not necessarily consistent with sick euthyroid.
Most notably, his TBG was within normal limits, as was his
uptake.  Thyroid peroxidase antibodies were sent to rule out
Hashimoto's thyroiditis and these were normal.  The patient
was ultimately given a trial of thyroxine IV given
consideration that hypothyroidism might be contributing to
his depressed mental status as well as to the other
hemodynamic abnormalities, which were more chronic.  Please
see cardiac discussion for response to thyroxine.  The
patient did not improve substantially after the
administration of IV thyroxine.  We also considered poor
oxygenation secondary to congestive heart failure as a
possible etiology.  Although the patient's oxygenation
remained relatively good, he showed some evidence of
congestive heart failure by chest x-ray and responded to
Lasix.  The patient had head CT, which showed no acute
change.  He ultimately underwent a lumbar puncture, which
showed no infectious process.  Ultimately, we attributed the
patient's depressed mental status to a resolving pulmonary
process, which may have tipped him over given his baseline
brain dysfunction.  As on prior admissions to the ICU, the
patient has taken up to a week to ten days to gradually
resolve.  It was not clear that the intervention on our part
reversed his neurological process.

Regarding his history of seizure disorder, phenobarbital
level was checked on admission to the Medical ICU and found
to be within normal limits.  As per the neurology
recommendations, we loaded him on phenobarbital and then
continued his prior dosing.  He was also continued on his
prior dose of Lamictal during this hospitalization.

#2.  PULMONARY:  The patient presented with evidence of right
and left basilar pneumonia thought to be aspiration given
history of recurrent aspiration.  He was treated with
Ceftriaxone and Clindamycin initially.  The patient did
develop a rash on his chest and upper arms later in his MICU
course, which looked consistent with a drug rash.  Given his
history of reaction to betalactams and Imipenem, it was
thought that the Ceftriaxone might be the culprit.  Base on
the results of his preliminary sputum cultures, we did not
have a alternative choice of treatment.  We did push him from
Clindamycin to Flagyl at that time out of consideration that
the Clindamycin might be the culprit.  His rash subsequently
resolved on its own, despite continued treatment with
Ceftriaxone.

The patient had thick and copious, often blood tinged
secretions throughout his hospitalization.  Sputum cultures
were consistently contaminated samples, but did grow moderate
Klebsiella and Citrobacter, both of which were multi-drug
resistant.  The Citrobacter species was initially read as
being sensitive to Ceftriaxone and Ceftriaxone was,
therefore, continued.  It was later read as being
intermediate sensitivity.  The Klebsiella was essentially
resistant to everything except for Imipenem and Amikacin.
Given his overall clinical improvement at that time and the
thought that the Klebsiella and the Citrobacter might be
chronic colonizers more than anything else, no further
antibiotics were added.  The patient will complete a 14-day
course of Ceftriaxone and Clindamycin/Flagyl.

Regarding his history of congestive heart failure, the
patient was noted to have a mild failure by chest x-ray with
some small bilateral effusions.  We did evaluate his right-
sided effusion during this hospitalization by ultrasound with
the expectation to perform a thoracentesis for possible
septic source contributing to his depressed mental status.
There were no significant tapable effusion noted by
ultrasound and that procedure was deferred.  The patient's
dry weight was determined to be 103 kg and he was given
p.r.n. Lasix to maintain him at that goal-body weight.
Congestive heart failure did not appear to be a major
contributor to the patient's deterioration during his
hospitalization.  Regarding the patient's history of chronic
obstructive pulmonary disease he was maintained on
bronchodilators and steroids.  He was initially given IV
hydrocortisone thinking that he might have panhypopituitarism
explaining his relative hypotension.  We ultimately
transitioned him to PO Prednisone for better
anti-inflammatory coverage of his chronic obstructive
pulmonary disease.  He should undergo a slow taper off the
Prednisone and he was at 30 mg p.o.q.d. at the time of
transfer to the floor.

#3.  CARDIOVASCULAR:  The patient has a history of coronary
artery disease, status post myocardial infarction, which is
not documented by recent catheterization or treadmill test on
our computer system.  The patient was continued on aspirin.
He would likely not tolerated beta blockade given his chronic
bradycardia.  The patient was noted to experience
approximately six hours rate-controlled atrial fibrillation
in the setting of IV Levoxyl therapy.  The patient was ruled
out for a myocardial infarction at this time.  Other possible
etiologies of his atrial fibrillation included chronic
obstructive pulmonary disease, and underlying infection.  We
felt that the most likely explanation was Levoxyl, which had
been started just a day or two prior.  The patient
subsequently reconverted to normal sinus rhythm within
approximately six hours and had no further episodes of atrial
fibrillation.  The Levoxyl was held for the remainder of the
hospitalization.

#4. RENAL:  The patient had a baseline creatinine of
approximately 0.7 to 0.8.  When the patient was initially
admitted to the floor, he was aggressively diuresed for
thought that his heart failure might be contributing to his
depressed mental status and difficulty breathing.  At that
time, the creatinine subsequently doubled from about 1.7 to
1.5.  The patient was subsequently allowed to re-equilibrate
with goal dry weight of 103 kg.  The patient was noted to be
relatively hypernatremic to the 150s during his MICU course.
At that time he was receiving nutrition in the form of tube
feeds.  We added t.i.d. free-water boluses and his sodium
subsequently normalized.

#5.  HEMATOLOGICAL:  The patient has chronically low
platelets and white blood count, which had been attributed to
a myelodysplastic syndrome.  The patient has a baseline
hematocrit of 29 to 30 and he was noted to slowly drift down
to approximately 25 by the day of transfer to the floor.  He
has had a single trace guaiac-positive stool on this
admission.  We will plan to recheck the hematocrit and
initiate a full anemia workup and consider transfusion given
his coronary artery disease history.  Regarding the
guaiac-positive stool, he should likely receive an outpatient
colonoscopy.

#6.  The patient was evaluated by the Endocrine Service and
through to have either secondary hypothyroidism versus sick
euthyroid syndrome.  The endocrine team felt that neither of
these diagnoses likely explain his acute deterioration in his
mental status.  It was difficult to interpret his thyroid
function tests given that all current prior tests had been
done in the setting of acute illness.  The Endocrine Service
has signed off and recommend a full panel of thyroid function
tests be done prior to discharge.  We held off on giving the
patient further thyroxine given the unclear diagnosis and
given the fact that he went into atrial fibrillation within
one day of administration.

#7.  FLUIDS, ELECTROLYTES, AND NUTRITION:  The patient was
maintained at a dry body weight of approximately 103 kg.  He
was given p.r.n. Lasix to keep him at that weight.  We
aggressively repleted his potassium, magnesium, and
phosphate.  He was followed by the nutrition service and kept
on tube feeds at goal.  We would recommend a repeat
swallowing study and revisiting the issue of a chronic PEG
tube with the family prior to discharge.

#8.  PROPHYLAXIS:  The patient was maintained on Protonix and
subcutaneous heparin.

#9.  ACCESS:  The patient had a right PIC line placed during
this hospitalization for the administration of antibiotics.
This can potentially be discontinued once the antibiotic
course is filled.  The patient's medications at the time of
transfer to the floor revealed the following:

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1.  Colace 100 mg per the NG tube b.i.d.
2.  Senokot one tablet po/pr/q.d.
3.  Lamictal 150 mg per the NG tube q.d.
4.  Phenobarbital 60 mg per the NG tube q.a.m.; 90 mg per the
NG tube q.p.m.
5.  Neurontin 300 mg per the NG tube t.i.d.
6.  Alphagan ophthalmic solution, one drop OS t.i.d.
7.  Flovent MDI two puffs inhaled b.i.d.
8.  Singular 10 mg p.o. per the NG tube q.d.
9.  Albuterol and Atrovent nebs q.2h. to 4h.p.r.n.
10. Xalatan eye drops, one drop to the left eye q.h.s.
11. Subcutaneous heparin 5000 units subcutaneously b.i.d.
12. Atrovent MDI 2 puffs q.i.d.
13. Respalor tube feeds to goal 55 cc per hour per the NG
tube .
14. Prednisone 30 mg per the G-tube q.d. to be slowly weaned.
15. Prevacid 30 cc per the NG tube q.d.
16. Flagyl 500 mg per the G tube q.8h. to be discontinued on
1984-06-20.
17. Aspirin 325 mg per the NG tube q.d.
18. Ceftriaxone 1 gram IV q.24 hours to be discontinued
1984-06-20.
19. Free water boluses 400 cc per the NG tube t.i.d.
20. Haldol 2.5 mg per the NG tube b.i.d.
21. Artificial tears one drop q.i.d.p.r.n.
22. Nystatin powder to all affected areas p.r.n.



Please see additional discharge summary/addendum for other
events of hospital course, as well as for final discharge
diagnoses and discharge medications.




                            DR.Christopher,Michael 12-929

Dictated By:Martin
MEDQUIST36

D:  1993-09-12  13:23
T:  1993-09-12  13:31
JOB#:  876
